emails.subject: Can I help checking on the status of the sabbatical specifically if or when they will request a j Visa?
emails.body:
Hey! Can you ask Columbia or just tell me who your contact is and I can
call or email them I'm happy to Do the leg work. Are they planning to or
have they submitted any requests for a J1 visa application? I just want to
know because I need to understand when I go across the border how the US
perceives me. It's totally fine or even better if they haven't.

Some universities submit this request for a visa on J1 but some don't. Also
I've had several experiences where universities aren't fully aware of how
to handle visas for certain cases and I got denied at the border a couple
times.

I will probably just let them know I don't need a j Visa, but it's probably
easier for me to just get in touch with them directly like call or email,
then it's less hassle for you

Joseph
{Sent from mobile phone with voice to text, please excuse any typos or
abruptness}

bio:
Lydia Chilton is an Associate Professor in the Computer Science Department at Columbia University. Her research focuses on human-centered AI -- developing principles for combining the best of human and AI abilities to enhance design, innovation, and problem-solving. Projects include AI code synthesis for interactive app creation, interactive animation authoring, creating media for journalism, helping scientists communicate their work to the public, assisting farmers in adapting to climate change, and simulating human behavior to inform social policy decisions.

emails.subject: AI Adoption and its impact in the workplace:
emails.body:
Good afternoon Dr. Chilton, hope you are doing well!

I am an assistant professor of radiology at CUMC with a research focus on adopting AI tools to non-invasively detect tumor architecture and genomic alterations, and to predict response to treatment. I recently received a foundation grant from the American Academy of Radiology to validate and implement modality-agnostic tools for brain tumor segmentation and radiogenomic classification tasks. One major part of my grant is to implement and evaluate these tools in a real-world clinical scenario simulation and understand how people react to these tools and what factors might improve their adoption.

I've reviewed some of your work and believe I could benefit from your guidance. If possible, I’d like to meet in person or via Zoom to discuss my projects and explore potential collaboration opportunities. Thank you in advance!

P.S: Attached below is my CV and Google Scholar link (https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__scholar.google.com_citations-3Fuser-3DMmyDV2EAAAAJ-26hl-3Den&d=DwIGaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=1tdLLL2Btl0-JFRRDL1SLCRQyKx3eKTMmAMWsGtTvtF6JH39RI_S-vuya4gMBqhy&s=9oH2aCY79jnW9xXh7cm5SdX6SEWGa9fsWQngxmQ7-F8&e= ).

Best regards,
Gagandeep Singh, MD
Assistant Professor of Radiology
Neuroradiology Division
Columbia University Irving Medical Center

    Join us for the opening of “Her Scent of Mystery,” an exhibition tracing the rediscovery of the "Scent of Mystery," a long-lost perfume created for the 1960 Smell-O-Vision film of the same name.

emails.subject: Re: Thank you
emails.body:
Dear Lydia,

I realize it's been a little while, but I wanted to loop back and see if
you might be open to chatting about potential collaborations sometime. I'll
actually be in New York from August 11–17 for an exhibition I'm working on,
and I'd love to meet up if you're around!

I'll be starting at MIT next July and am using this in-between period to
reflect on the directions I'd like my group to pursue. Our previous
conversation left a strong impression, and I'd love to explore possible
overlaps in our work.

Hope all is well on your end, and looking forward to hearing from you!
(CC'ing my MIT email just to be safe.)

Sincerely,
Jas

emails.subject: EMNLP 2025 has received your submission titled OPeRA: A Dataset of Observation, Persona, Rationale, and Action for Evaluating LLMs on Human Online Shopping Behavior Simulation
emails.body:
Your submission to EMNLP 2025 has been posted.

Submission Number: 4356 

Title: OPeRA: A Dataset of Observation, Persona, Rationale, and Action for Evaluating LLMs on Human Online Shopping Behavior Simulation 

Abstract: Can Large Language models (LLMs) accurately simulate the next web action of a specific user? While LLMs have shown promising capabilities in generating believable human behaviors, evaluating their ability to mimic real user behaviors remains an open challenge, largely due to the lack of high-quality, publicly available datasets that capture both the observable actions and the internal reasoning of an actual human user. To address this gap, we introduce OPeRA, a novel dataset of Observation, Persona, Rationale, and Action collected from real human participants during online shopping sessions. OPeRA is the first public dataset that comprehensively captures: user personas, browser observations, fine-grained web actions, and self-reported just-in-time rationales. We developed both an online questionnaire and a custom browser plugin to gather this dataset with high fidelity. Using OPeRA, we establish the first benchmark to evaluate how well current LLMs can predict a specific user’s next action and rationale with a given persona and <observation, action, rationale> history. This dataset lays the groundwork for future research into LLM agents that aim to act as personalized digital twins for human. 

To view your submission, click here: https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__openreview.net_forum-3Fid-3DtUuo8GQB1n&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=SjxA6zlqWFK7bYNy2JFyhhoWpMy42cN1JNxfICuuIKtXpHXnnpOmZhA6cgQdP7Sb&s=WkdWi5BQQaTdz2eBbFSZEy1Uv62NorosnLMHko8dCLU&e= 

If you are not an author of this submission and would like to be removed, please contact the author who added you at wangziyi000128@gmail.com

emails.body:
https://click.m.columbia.edu/?qs=d145a6dfde589da8f89f0a33448529bb7f5796208cd2982c2908b4536afe4106f7292d63b825ca80e11b2282bc64d945d7dc1b7962223c02  

 August 1, 2025

Dear members of the Columbia community,

Yesterday, I had the chance to spend part of the day at Columbia’s oasis-like campus in Rockland County, the Lamont-Doherty Earth Observatory. For 75 years, scientists there have been seeking fundamental knowledge about the origin, evolution, and future of the natural world, which has yielded unparalleled scientific discovery in areas such as plate tectonics, ice age theory, El Nino prediction, and carbon capture. Listening to the next generation of scientists, in addition to our Climate School dean, Dr. Alexis Abramson; Dr. Steve Goldstein, Lamont’s interim director; and Dr. David Goldberg, the deputy director, I was reminded, as always, of the excellence that is Columbia. I also found Lamont emblematic of a unique and core Columbia ethos, one which is deeply
 interdisciplinary, and one in which questions of real-world translation are often asked alongside the pursuit of fundamental knowledge.

Dr. Ajit Subramaniam, who focuses on phytoplankton physiology, among other things, is also engaged in a long-running project with native communities in Alaska to better understand marine ecosystem changes that they are experiencing. Dr. Marco Tedesco’s work spans research on ice sheet dynamics to the economics of climate change, and he also works with both the Data Science Institute and the Business School. Dr. Yutian Wu discussed interactions between the atmosphere and Arctic sea ice and teaches environmental policy at the School of International and Public Affairs. Dr. Jacky Austermann, whose work focuses on the development and melting of continental ice sheets and their effect on land elevation and sea level through time to better understand our present and future climate, also
 leads projects in Greenland to help coastal communities plan for their ch

emails.subject: Thoughts on Sitong's project
emails.body:
Hi Lydia,

Since I’ll miss Monday’s project meeting, I want to share my perspective on Sitong’s work and hear your thoughts.

Vision. The recent pivot is to build a system that generates personalized designs. We’re not chasing a general single “best” design; instead, we aim to help each user navigate the design space; show what’s possible for their content, highlight trade-offs, and let them compare realistic variations. Our technical contribution focuses on (1) defining a targeted design space from user input and (2) proposing plausible alternatives the user can select.

Linear chat is powerful, but visual design is nonlinear and, well, visual. Sitong’s current interface on top of GPT-Image as a richer interface feels promising. (If you haven’t seen our internal MAX Sneaks submission, I’ll ask her to share—it tells the story well, though we’ll need more detail and broader scope for a research paper.)

The same framework could extend to Veo 3. I’ve seen strong results using Veo 3 for storytelling in other intern projects; it translates detailed prompts into realistic video much like GPT-Image did for stills. I wonder if we can do the same for motion graphics or other video-things.

I suggest we give Sitong the next few weeks to build out the prototype and fully test this direction before considering a pause or pivot.

What do you think? I’d love your perspective. Also, welcome back to NY! I hope you’re getting some summer weather before it disappears!

Best,
Ding

emails.subject: Cracked REGULAR CONFLICTS PROMPT
emails.body:
Here is a description of an image we are making a funny caption for:
${description}
People and locations in this image include ${celebrity}

List 20 possible, 5-10 word, relatable scenarios each in which conflicts
 or narratives can be established in relatable settings such as conflicts
 with my boss, people acting childish, people being too nerdy, being broke,
 being a poser, online dating, capitalism, competition. List at least one
scenario for each conflict type.

 When coming up with humor, coming up
 with assumptions about the subject is key.
If there isn't a person in the image, you should anthropomorphize the
objects.
 Think before writing what would be a relatable scenario, narrative, or
 assumption based on the image.
 The scenarios should not be related to the image, but rather, taking
 elements of the image and applying common scenarios to them.
 Don't write any intros, just send the original response.

emails.subject: Fwd: UIST 2025 Doctoral Symposium: Final Review of Submission 1052
emails.body:
OK they sent the feedback for DS! At least I get the validation that the
idea is good and the submission is well-written :-) I do agree I should
make it clearer of how schema relates to design patterns. I will at least
put it on my website / arxiv it.

And thank you so much for the great suggestions for the Adobe proj! For the
next steps,
I think I will still start off from still images for the prototype, but
maybe modulate the code so it's easier to change to other media types -
like video/audio.
I believe the fundamental thing won't change - analyze the design logic,
help people find what matters, keep them the same, and explore around it.
As I said I think the reviewing UI might need to change if it's not an
image, as I know it's harder to compare if it's video/audio.
I am right now thinking the system more as a* "example-based design agent"*,
it should follow the same fundamental planning logic, but doesn't have to
follow the same workflow using the same set of tools (like just GPT
imageGen).

Let me know if you have any thoughts. Will keep you posted! :-)

*Sitong Wang*
Columbia University, PhD student in Computer Science
sw3504@columbia.edu

Hope you are doing well! 

It has been almost one year anniversary since we last chatted!
Many things have happened since last time. I, of course, pivoted! I’ve been  
working on an AI-native enterprise collaboration platform—something that  
feels like coming full circle, as my PhD dissertation focused on enterprise  
collaboration. It’s a space that’s close to my heart and also my gem. I  
have found a co-founder: a CS professor at Columbia University whom I’ve  
known for years. Despite knowing each other for so long, it was only

emails.subject: Brian Harvey, Teaching Professor Emeritus, University of California at Berkeley
emails.body:
On the inspiration behind his computer center at Lincoln-Sudbury Regional High School, the success of the Beauty and Joy of Computing curriculum, why grades are "evil," and more

August 5, 2025

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Brian Harvey is Teaching Professor Emeritus at the University of California at Berkeley. His interests include computer science education, curricula, programming languages, and visual languages. Harvey is the author of Computer Science Logo Style, a three-volume computer programming textbook which uses the Logo programming language, and Simply Scheme (with Matthew Wright), an introduction to computer programming for non-majors. With his Berkeley colleague Dan Garcia, Harvey developed the Beauty and Joy of Computing (BJC) (https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__bjc.berkeley.edu_&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=-_LS5uo2o7-geJ5gnr0XLcB_sGrtzBYehOspkA3M8gk&m=b4j1IgXr31-d4tVosMY1aJtEf4OKIFX4sT13A2_0IdnJgxq9_WqVYuI3l0ShxesJ&s=xkum6i2CsBq-iSMeQ1dbGPn2gbzQQnpkAnIGh7RXEm0&e= ) curriculum, which began as a national pilot for the CSforAll movement (https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__csforall.org_&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=-_LS5uo2o7-geJ5gnr0XLcB_sGrtzBYehOspkA3M8gk&m=b4j1IgXr31-d4tVosMY1aJtEf4OKIFX4sT13A2_0IdnJgxq9_WqVYuI3l0ShxesJ&s=tWwaejpd950p0z6K6A2UnaXacvGOCTkZvuSu_XOW8gY&e= ).

Harvey recently received (with Garcia) the ACM Karl V. Karlstrom Outstanding Educator Award (https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__awards.acm.org_karlstrom&d=DwIFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=-_LS5uo2o7-geJ5gnr0XLcB_sGrtzBYehOspkA3M8gk&m=b4j1IgXr31-d4tVosMY1aJtEf4OKIFX4sT13A2_0IdnJgxq9_WqVYuI3l0ShxesJ&s=ThPGQ0byZ0drdYmcszPm8rRiP5BF0foEuUJnph80Ppw&e= ) for his work to bring computing to all students, especially those from historically underrepresented communities.

[ACM] We'

emails.subject: DES Fall 2025 Registration yd2583
emails.body:
Dear Yulun,

I hope this email finds you well. I’m writing to confirm your progress in
the DES program. Based on your program progress, you should register for 3
points of COMS 9911 to satisfy the continuous enrollment requirement. Your
thesis proposal and defense are your outstanding requirements.

Please carefully review the requirements and your academic record below.
Let me know if you see any discrepancies.

*Coursework Status: **Complete*

·      4 Distribution Courses

·      6 Electives

·      If CSOR 4231 was not taken at Columbia, it was imported to count for
the prerequisite: *Yes*

*60 Total Points Status: **60** points earned*

·      Includes a maximum of 30 points from a terminal master’s degree.

o   MS must be reflected in SSOL. No action needed if MS was done at
Columbia University.

·      Excludes any points earned in COMS 9800.

*COMS 9800 Directed Research: **12** points taken out of 12.*

·      12 total points taken at a pace of 3 points per semester for four
semesters.

*Teaching Requirement Status: **EXEMPT - CVN-DES** out of 2 units*

·      Two teaching units that must be completed in person.

*Candidacy Exam: **Passed*

*Thesis Proposal:  **(Next)*

*Thesis Defense: **(Future)*

Again, please let me know if you have any questions.

Best,

Da’Shante

*Da'Shante Smith*
Associate Director of Academic Administration and Student Services
Computer Science | Fu Foundation School of Engineering and Applied Science
